coredata.dc:description: Disproportionate use of antibiotics and pesticides is a bone-chilling concern for the environment and humans as well. So, it is essential to monitor the contamination caused by antibiotics and pesticides using different efficient detection techniques. In this regard, electrochemical sensors have proved to be a powerful tool for detecting pesticides and antibiotics due to their various advantages including synergistic aspects, systematized fabrication procedures, easier detection, remarkable sensitivity and selectivity. This review discusses the fabrication of different electrochemical sensors for detecting antibiotics and pesticides in different analytes (food and water samples) using different carbon-based electrodes such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, and its oxide, nanomaterials with molecularly imprinted polymers, and various miscellaneous carbon materials – carbon dots, carbon nanofiber, carbon nanodiamond, etc. Finally, the review includes a look at the future concepts in this research area to assist in improving the detection techniques for antibiotics and pesticides.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Disproportionate use of antibiotics and pesticides is a bone-chilling concern for the environment and humans as well. So, it is essential to monitor the contamination caused by antibiotics and pesticides using different efficient detection techniques. In this regard, electrochemical sensors have proved to be a powerful tool for detecting pesticides and antibiotics due to their various advantages including synergistic aspects, systematized fabrication procedures, easier detection, remarkable sensitivity and selectivity. This review discusses the fabrication of different electrochemical sensors for detecting antibiotics and pesticides in different analytes (food and water samples) using different carbon-based electrodes such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, and its oxide, nanomaterials with molecularly imprinted polymers, and various miscellaneous carbon materials – carbon dots, carbon nanofiber, carbon nanodiamond, etc. Finally, the review includes a look at the future concepts in this research area to assist in improving the detection techniques for antibiotics and pesticides.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics and pesticides are unequivocally the materials that are widely used in medicinal and agricultural fields respectively. The use of both materials is unavoidable in their respective fields. Pesticides form the backbone of Indian agriculture by increasing agricultural production to meet growing population needs [1]. Nonetheless, excessive use of pesticides contaminates the groundwater, gets accumulated in food, causing a serious threat to humans and the environment [2,3]. Antibiotics play important role in human life due to their antiseptic and anti-inflammatory actions. But using it for a longer duration of time creates harmful effects on both humans and the environment. Long-time exposure to the antibiotic in the environment inhibits the growth of bacteria, it also affects animals and foods by increasing the risk of cancer [4]. Thus, the fact that intensive use of pesticides and antibiotics may affect critically both environment, as well as living beings cannot be neglected. Thus, the detection of both analytes becomes vital to save the biosphere.

Methods conventionally used to test pesticides and antibiotics include gas chromatography (GC), liquid chromatography (LC), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS), and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS). Though these analytical techniques provide selective and sensitive results, they also suffer from various disadvantages like it requires highly trained technicians, more time, more cost, and highly sophisticated instruments. These limitations make the above techniques unsuited for pesticide and antibiotic determination in various samples [5]. Thus there is a need for developing a sensor system that is highly stable, selective, reliable and cost-effective. Electrochemical sensors emerge to be a good alternative overcoming the several disadvantages of the traditional methods used. Electrochemical sensors are cheaper, user-friendly, provide faster detection, high sensitivity and selectivity for detecting antibiotics and pesticides [6]. The electrochemical sensors also share several other advantages as it provides a lower detection limit and has simple construction and practicality. The electrochemical behaviour of biomolecules and medicines and the advancements in electrochemical measuring systems contributed to an ever-increasing interest by researchers in electrochemical sensors for drug, food, environmental and agricultural analysis [7]. Electrochemical sensors can be classified into five types: conductometric sensors, potentiometric sensors, impedimetric sensors, amperometric sensors, and voltammetric sensors. Among them, voltammetric techniques (including cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV), and square wave voltammetry (SWV)) and amperometric techniques have been widely applied to environmental analysis [8].

There are numerous applications for nanomaterials, including environmental analysis, food testing, medical care, and pharmaceutical analysis. Carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) are one type of nanomaterial that offers many advantages including ease of preparation, good reproducibility, high stability, low ohmic resistivity, and robustness. Different allotropes of carbon like nanodiamonds, carbon dots (C-dots), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon nanofibers (CNFs), and graphene are included under the carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) family [9,10,12–14]. These materials have certain outstanding properties including chemical inertness, more surface area and reaction site availability, and excellent electrocatalytic activity and conductivity which attracts researchers' attention for their use in electrochemical sensor applications [15]. An electrochemical active area modification can significantly improve an electrochemical sensor's sensitivity and selectivity. Although the bare electrodes are useful for many electrochemical reactions, there are some disadvantages such as lack of reproducibility of the surface behaviour, slower reactions for some compounds on the electrode surface, and overpotential requirements [16]. Various materials like CNTs, CNFs, graphene and its oxide can be used for modifying electrodes surface[17]. Recently, it is observed that carbon materials like CNTs, graphene, and its oxide, carbon black are emerging to be outstanding materials for electrochemical sensors due to their ability to increase the electron transfer rate which ultimately results in higher sensitivity of the sensor. Literature shows that CNMs including CNTs, graphene, and its oxide, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) based methods are proving to be good candidates for electrochemical detection.

Due to their versatility and diverse application in sensing strategies, CNTs have drawn increasing interest [18]. CNTs are classified as single-wall CNTs (SWCNTs) and multi-wall CNTs (MWCNTs). SWCNTs and MWCNTs enhance the electron transfer rate due to these properties they are widely used for modifying electrodes for the fabrication of various electrochemical sensors [19].

Graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) are commonly used in electrochemical sensors because of their ease of fabrication, cost-effectiveness, scalability, and high solubility in water. Furthermore, GO/rGO thin films are highly electrochemically efficient as well as ligand-binding, due to high electrical conductivity, redox properties, stability, oxygen groups, and topological defects, including holes, graphitic regions, and high contrast disorder [20].

MIP is prepared by functional and cross-linking polymerization in the presence of a target molecule (template), which produces structure, size, and stereospecific cavities, followed by the removal of the template from those cavities, resulting in the rebinding of the target. Literature shows that carbon-based electrochemical sensors show rapid and highly sensitive recognition behaviour towards the target compound. This fact has led to many studies investigating MIP-modified carbon electrodes, such as GO, MWCNTs, glassy carbon electrodes, and carbon paste electrodes. It is because carbon electrodes have excellent conductivity, high surface areas, and mechanical stability, whereas MIPs provides selectivity [6].

This review aims to address the applications of different CNMs including CNTs, graphene and its oxide, MIP-based carbon materials and miscellaneous carbon materials for developing electrochemical sensors for detecting pesticides and antibiotics in different food and environmental samples.

Electrochemical sensors are device that detects the target substance either qualitatively or quantitatively. It works on the principle that when a measured substance reacts with a specific sensing element and undergoes a redox reaction resulting generation of a signal which is proportional to the target substance concentration. Fig. 1 represents how an electrochemical sensor works. The complete setup of the electrochemical sensor includes a piece of equipment for electrochemical sensing, working, reference and counter electrodes, and an electrolyte where the reaction takes place [21].

Antibiotics are natural, synthetic, or semi-synthetic compounds capable of killing or inhibiting the growth of different bacteria, archaea, viruses, protozoa, microalgae, and fungi [22,23]. The classification of antibiotics is represented in Fig. 2. The common application of antibiotics as antibacterial agents is to treat human or animal infections [24]. By contrast, the excessive use of antibiotics during production and application may cause antibiotic residue to linger in the environment, potentially harming flora and fauna as well as negatively impacting food processing, livestock stocking, and public health. By contrast, the excessive use of antibiotics during production and application may cause antibiotic residue to linger in the environment, potentially.
harming flora and fauna as well as negatively impacting food processing, livestock stocking, and public health [26].

CNTs excellent structural, optical, and electrical properties, making them very suitable for the detection of pesticides and antibiotics [27]. In addition to acting as a highly conductive material, CNTs increase the roughness of the electrode surface, which in turn increases the number of imprinted sites [28]. The literature discusses the role of CNTs as electrode material for several electrochemical sensors [29].

Olaquindox (OLA) is a type of synthetic antibacterial drug that is often added to poultry and swine feeds as a medicinal additive [30]. But at the same time, it also shows various mutagenic and carcinogenic effects [31]. In a study, a new electrochemical sensor based on superb pure single-walled carbon nanotubes (spSWCNTs) was constructed to detect the antibiotic OLA. The synthesized material was characterized using SEM, TEM, XPS, and FT-IR whereas electrochemical studies were conducted using CV and DPV analysis. The intensity of the peak current observed was 1700 times more than the bare gold electrode which represented the strong electrocatalytic properties of OLA on the modified electrode. Small charge-transfer resistance was observed for spSWCNTs modified electrode, which again reveals the enhanced electrocatalytic property of spSWCNTs modified electrode [19].

A common antibiotic used in animal farms both therapeutically and prophylactically is sulfamethoxazole (SMX). Due to the inability of SMX to dissolve, it accumulates in food (e.g. milk), increasing allergies in consumers and favouring the development of antimicrobial resistance [28,32–34]. In another study, a novel electrochemical sensor was developed using screen-printed electrodes modified with MWCNT and decorated with Prussian blue nanocubes (MWCNT/PBnc) for detecting antibiotics. In a screen-printed electrode, all three electrodes -- working, counter, and reference are mounted on a single chip [35]. Low charge transfer resistance values of MWCNT/PBnc/SPE obtained from the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy study indicate the faster electron transfer process on the electrodes surface. Thus, the prepared sensor successfully detected the antibiotics SMX and TMP simultaneously[32]. Another study reports the fabrication of electrochemical sensors for detecting hydroxylamine where promazine hydrochloride (PHC) was used as a homogeneous mediator and MWCNT was used to enhance the carbon paste electrode surface. An electrochemical study done using CV and LSV showed that the fabricated sensor was highly selective and sensitive [36].

A novel electrochemical sensor was developed using MWCNT coated GCE and foamed gold electrodes for the detection of cefiderocol antibiotics for drug monitoring. This technique was used as a point of care sensor for detecting the concentration of cefiderocol in blood samples. MWCNT-coated pyrolytic carbon electrodes were tested in blood samples. The sensitivity of the modified electrode was greatly enhanced as compared to the unmodified one [37].

Ofloxacin antibiotic is widely prescribed for treating infectious diseases in humans and animals. Nonetheless, excessive use of ofloxacin may lead to human health problems like allergies and drug resistance [38]. In a study, the detection of ofloxacin was done using an electrochemical sensor which was made by blending carbon paste electrode with flake graphite (Fg) and oxidized/functionalized MWCNT. A superior interest is fostered in the electrochemical field by MWCNTs and graphite, due to their excellent electro-catalytic properties, electrical conductivity, high active surface areas, and excellent mechanical, chemical, electronic, and thermal stability [39–41]. Determination of ofloxacin was done in human urine samples using square wave adsorptive anodic stripping voltammetry. Sensors prepared by blending carbon paste with 10.0 % flake graphite and 5.0 % oxidized multiwall carbon nanotubes showed the best result. The sensor prepared was thus found to be highly stable and reusable [42].

One recent study describes the development of a GCE modified with a composite material that combines the high electrical conductivity and large surface area of CNTs with the catalytic sites of WS2. The composite material was characterized by Raman spectroscopy, XRD, and SEM. Thus, the proposed electrochemical sensor had the promising potential for determining isoniazid in biological samples [43].

MWCNTs are known to facilitate faster electron transfer reactions between the electrode and its interface. Moreover, MWCNTs have unique mechanical, electronic, and structural properties that make them promising for applications in a range of sensing technologies [44]. Thus it can be concluded that CNTs are catching attention in sensing pharmaceutical pollutants due to highly effective surface area, mass transfer, and chemical and electrical conductivity. Applications of carbon nanotubes for antibiotic detection are illustrated in Table 1.

The unique properties of carbon composites make them an effective adsorbent in the adsorption process, such as their high surface area, increased porosity, increased adsorption capacity, high surface reactivity, and bigger pores [45]. These composites are formed using activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, or graphene. Graphene and its derivatives have unique properties like more surface area, enhanced conductivity, good mechanical strength, and mass production which provides a 2D space for electron transfer reactions to occur [46–48]. It is observed that graphene and reduced graphene has higher conductivity as compared to graphene oxide due to fewer defects on the surface [49]. Due to the interesting properties of graphene, it is widely used as an electrocatalyst in sensors, electronic devices, and energy storage devices.

In a study, an electrochemical sensor was fabricated using graphene and ZnO nanorods modified GCE detecting sulfamethoxazole (SMX) and trimethoprim (TMP) antibiotics. ZnO shares several unique properties like a less toxic nature, and thermally stable, and shows good results for electrochemical detection [50]. The morphological and structural studies of ZnO nanorods were done by SEM and XRD. ZnO nanorods formed were smooth and in cylindrical shape. XRD results showed that ZnO nanorods had a hexagonal wruzite phase. The sensor showed a linearity range of 1–220 μmol/L with LOD 0.4 μmol/L for SMX and a linearity range of 1–180 μmol/L with LOD 0.3 μmol/L for TMP. The sensor showed good application in environmental protection as well as in the medicinal field [4].

Nitrofurantoin (NFT) drug is known for its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic action. Though it has therapeutic actions on the other side it also causes side effects to the human body such as liver damage, kidney damage, nausea, etc [51]. A fascinating study was done for detecting NFT using Lutetium vanadate/graphene sheet nanocomposite LuVO4/GRS. Physical characterizations of the LuVO4/GRS nanocomposite were carried out using XRD, Raman spectroscopy, XPS, FESEM, HRTEM and EDX. The electrochemical measurements done using CV analysis indicated excellent activity of LuVO4/GRS towards NFT. It was found that the developed sensor showed high sensitivity and selectivity for detecting NFT antibiotics with a limit of detection of 0.001 μM [52].

Copper(I) sulphide nanomaterials come up with various electrochemical applications due to their excellent electrochemical properties. In a recent study, Cu2S nanosphere decorated reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite (rGO@Cu2S NC) was used for electrochemical detection of chloramphenicol (CAP) antibiotics in milk. Preparation of the nanocomposite was done using the sonochemical method. Cu2S NPs were evenly distributed on the surface of reduced graphene oxide in RGO@Cu2S nanocomposite. CV results indicate a larger redox peak response for the modified composite formed which means that RGO@Cu2S NC has enhanced electrocatalytic properties. A larger active surface area was calculated for the nanocomposite formed using the Randles–Sevcik equation that is associated with the synergic effect between the layered structure of rGO and Cu2S NPs. The modified electrode was inspected using an amperometric method for different concentrations of CAP. For practical use, the prepared sensor was analyzed in fresh milk and various milk products and it showed good results. Fig. 3 illustrates a stepwise synthesis of RGO@Cu2S NC and its electrochemical detection of CAP [53].

Another study reports the detection of chloramphenicol (CAP) using a nanocomposite of graphene oxide and palladium nanoparticles (rGO/PdNPs). Formation of rGO/PdNPs was confirmed by TEM, UV–Vis, FT-IR spectra and XPS. The electrochemical characteristics of the fabricated sensor were evaluated by CV and DPV analysis. In the electrochemical detection of CAP, the nanocomposite showed excellent electroactivity, a higher sensitivity, and excellent repeatability. The sensor showed good performance with linearity of 50–1000 nM and LOD 50 nM [54].

Recently, a paper-based eco-friendly electrochemical sensor was developed using reduced graphene nanoribbons (rGNR) for detecting SMX and TMP. Parchment paper has proved a good material for its use as a substrate in electrochemical sensors because of its properties including non-toxicity, flexibility, and resistivity to fluids [55,56]. Contact angle analysis showed that the paper has a hydrophilic character which is desired for a high ink transfer rate. The parchment paper used in the screen-printed carbon electrode showed good chemical and structural stability for its use in aqueous solutions at different pH. An electrochemical study done using DPV showed that the use of rGNR increased the sensitivity and peak current for detecting the two antibiotics simultaneously [57].

Cefixime (CEF) is a semi-synthetic material that is used for treating various infectious and bacterial diseases [58]. A novel electrochemical sensor was developed for detecting cefixime by modifying the GCE with graphene oxide and gold nanowires after which its surface was electropolymerized using a molecular imprinted polymeric layer of polyaniline. SEM images showed that the MIP layer has a more rough surface with deep pores as compared to the unextracted mode. The charge transfer resistance value was greater for GO/GCE as compared to the bare GCE which shows that modification with GO decreases the electron transfer. Before the extraction of CEF molecules in the imprinted cavities of MIP/GCW/GO/GCE the probe molecules were blocked from reaching the GCE surface, thus there was an increase in resistance. Thus the developed sensor showed satisfactory results for the detection of CEF in real samples of serum and urine [59].

A screen-printed carbon electrode modified with gold (Au) nanorods and GO was used in another study to detect cloxacillin (Clox) antibiotics using an electrochemical nanosensor. The prepared nanosensor and synthesised carbon nanocomposites were then characterized using commonly used techniques such as SEM, EDAX, BET, and FT-IR. The presence of Au and GO enhances the electron transfer rate (reducing charge transfer resistance) on the electrode's surface which further increases the sensitivity of the electrodes for detecting Clox molecules. Carbon composite with SWCNT is found to have more cavities and porous structures which are useful for the removal of the Clox molecule in this study. It was found that adding SWCNTs to carbon nanocomposites enhanced their texture properties significantly. The linearity range obtained was 5.0–775.0 nM with a detection limit of 1.6nM. Hence the prepared sensor was found suitable for detecting Clox [60].

Thus, it can be inferred from the above study that the large voltage window of graphene permits rapid electron transport rates, reducing charge transport resistance and enhancing the electrochemical effectiveness of the sensors. In recent years, graphene has gained much attention for its use in antimicrobial electrochemical sensors due to its large specific surface area, biocompatibility and efficient electron transport properties [61]. Thus, as a result of its high electroconductivity, large specific surface area, rapid heterogeneous electron transfer rate, low fouling, and low cost of graphite raw material, graphene has been identified as the most promising sensing material for antibiotic detection [62]. Table 2 compiles the different sensing studies of graphene and its oxide for detecting antibiotics.

Several MIPs are used as electrocatalysts due to their numerous favourable properties like simple instrumentation, cost-effectiveness, high selectivity, and sensitivity [63–65]. MIP shows the same structural properties as antibodies thus they are named plastic antibodies. It is observed that the use of nanomaterials with MIP not only increases the surface area of the sensor but also enhances electronic communication with the imprinted cavities [66]. MIPs are known for their selective properties, robust nature, low cost, and ease of synthesis on any sensor surface, and are the perfect recognition layers to be combined with electrochemical sensors for determining a variety of environmental analytes [67]. This section discusses the role of MIP in the electrochemical detection of various classes of antibiotics.

Azithromycin (AZT) is a macrolide antibiotic that helps treat a wide variety of gram-negative and gram-positive infections of the respiratory, ear, and skin. In spite of a high level of excretion into wastewater, only 14 % of this antibiotic is removed by wastewater treatment [68]. In a study, an electrochemical sensor was fabricated by electropolymerization of aniline on GCE which was further modified using graphene oxide and gold nanourchin for the detection of AZT. As a result of the fabrication of the MIP/GNU/GO electrode, the electrode-specific surface area was increased and its electron transfer rate and sensitivity were further enhanced. The practical application of the sensor was checked by performing DPV in blood serum. Thus the fabricated sensor was successfully able to detect AZT in different biological samples [69].

Cloxacillin antibiotic is used for treating microbial infections caused by Staphylococcus species, and various skin infections. In one of the methods, direct detection of cloxacillin was done by developing an electrochemical nanosensor using SPCE which was modified with MIP/GO/gold nanoparticles nanocomposite. It was observed that MIPs exhibited stronger adsorption selectivity toward cloxacillin compared to NIPs, even in the presence of other compounds. As a result of their high capacity, synthesized MIP particles can be used to effectively reduce the presence of drug residue in dairy samples. EIS study revealed that upon modification with GO the Rct value decreases as compared to the bare SPCE. The fabricated sensor showed good electrocatalytic activity and was found highly sensitive for detecting cloxacillin [70].

One of the recent study discusses the fabrication of molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensors for the detection of tetracycline (TCL) antibiotics using three-dimensional N, S co-doped lignin-based carbon microsphere/graphene composite hydrogel (GH-NSCMS) as an electrode material. The fabricated MIP electrochemical sensor for TCL detection was highly sensitive with a linear range 0.1–50 µM and LOD value 5 × 10-8 mol L-1 [71]. Thus the above discussed study reveals that a molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor combines the advantages of MIP with electrochemical sensing. There are several advantages on it, including its high sensitivity, ease of use, low detection limit, and strong selectivity. Furthermore, CNMs can also be used to modify electrodes to improve their response characteristics, such as sensitivity and specificity, and enhance electron transfer across interfaces. Table 3 lists various applications of MIPs for detecting antibiotics.

Apart from the carbon materials that are discussed in previous sections, there are few more carbon materials like carbon nanofiber, nanodiamond, carbon black and carbon nanoballs which also play important role in the electrochemical detection of certain classes of antibiotics. Some of these are discussed in this section.

The detection of pyrazinamide (PZA) antibiotics was done using nanodiamond-based electrochemical sensors. Since 1980, PZA drugs is used for the treatment of tuberculosis [72,73] but their use over the proposed level can lead to liver damage, arthralgia, nausea and vomiting, dysuria, malaise, fever, and sideroblastic anaemia [73]. Thus, it becomes very important to control PZA levels in the human body. In this study, the modification of GCE was done using nanodiamonds (ND), and sensing was performed using SWV. To characterize the ND film, scanning and transmission electron microscopy were used, and potassium ferricyanide was used as a redox probe in electrochemical characterization on the bare GCE and modified ND-GCE. It was observed that modified GCE showed higher electroactive surface area, and electroactive rate constant (k0). Thus the results obtained using this sensor indicate that it can be successfully used for the determination of PZA antibiotics [74].

Carbon black has attracted attention for its use as a modified electrode in recent years. It is formed by the incomplete burning of carbon or by pyrolysis of carbon materials. Carbon black shares several properties like high conductivity, chemical stability and formation of stable dispersion which favours its use in electrochemical studies [75]. In one such study, an electrochemical sensor was developed to detect antibiotics erythromycin, azithromycin, clarithromycin, and roxithromycin using Super P carbon black (SPCB) particles and chitosan (CT) as an electrode material. The fabricated electrochemical sensor was combined with batch injection analysis using amperometric detection (BIA-AMP). CT was used as a binding material for providing strong adhesion. It was inferred from the EIS study that a larger arc area was observed for bare GCE than the fabricated electrode SPCB-CT/GCE which means that the modified electrode showed less electron transfer resistance. The prepared sensor gave good results for the detection of four antibiotics in water and pharmaceutical samples [76].

In another study, Printex 6 L Carbon nanoballs (CNB) were used as the electrode material for fabricating electrochemical sensors for the detection of hydroquinone (HQ) and paracetamol (PARA). The morphology and structure of CNB were characterized using XRD, EDX, HR-TEM and Raman analysis whereas the conductive behaviour of the CNB was confirmed by CV and EIS study. The developed sensor showed enhanced performance when compared to CNT and graphene. As a result of CNB preparation procedures, cheaper materials can be obtained, as well as the amount/number of reagents and residues consumed can be reduced [77].

An interesting study was done using a gold modified electrode (CNF@AuNPs) as the working electrode for fabricating the electrochemical sensors for the detection of antibiotic metronidazole. This high electroreduction reaction of CNF@AuNPs to metronidazole is primarily attributed to the large surface areas and the high conductivity of CNF and AuNPs. The developed sensor was found to be highly sensitive and selective for the detection of antibiotic metronidazole in tap water samples [78].

In a study, a novel electrochemical sensor was developed using iron-nitrogen co-doped ordered mesoporous carbon-silicon nanocomposite (Si-Fe/NOMC) for detecting antibiotic chloramphenicol (CAP). The Si-Fe/NOMC modified GCE was made by placing Si-Fe/NOMC in an aqueous solution of isopropanol; this suspension was then dropped on the clean GCE and allowed to dry at room temperature. Electrochemical characterization using CV reveals that the presence of NOMC materials with silicon, nitrogen, silicon, and carbon enhances CAP reduction. The straight line is observed in the EIS curve of Si-Fe/NOMC/GCE which shows that the redox probe shows faster electron transfer kinetics and has better conductivity. The prepared sensor was successfully able to determine CAP in eye drop samples [79]. Table 4 illustrates different applications of miscellaneous carbon electrodes for detecting antibiotics.

The growing global population of the world demands more agricultural production which in turn requires the intensive use of pesticides. World health organization describes pesticides as substances that control the growth of pests or any undesirable species during the time of generation, transportation, distribution, and processing of the food [80–83]. Pesticides are classified as herbicides, fungicides, insecticides, and rodenticides [84]. They are often classified based on their chemistry, mode of action, or their target [72].

Organophosphate pesticides (OP) are the most commonly used pesticides among various classes of pesticides known. It is widely used in agricultural industries due to its ability to resist the growth of pests in different crops but at the same time, it also shares the drawback of attacking the nervous system of humans. The studies show that the toxicity of OP is due to the formation of PO bonds when it reacts with different molecules and metabolites [85]. When food containing OPs is consumed for a longer duration it causes great damage to the human body leading to gastrointestinal diseases, weakening the immune system, cancer, and sometimes also leading to death [86]. The various harmful effects of different classes of pesticides on a human being are summarized in Fig. 3. ( Fig. 4).

Studies show that SWCNTs and MWCNTs are used as significant materials for the electrochemical detection of pesticides in different samples. The MWCNTs are composed of tubular graphene sheets, which provides them with high surface area, high porosity, and improved chemical properties [87]. Studies also reveal that MWCNTs can also be used as r-DSPE sorbent for removing the sample residue in pesticide residue analysis [88]. An MWCNT has admirable mechanical, electrochemical, optical and chemical properties, as well as a large specific surface area and strong adsorption capabilities; thus, they have received considerable attention in materials science [89].

Various international health organizations have established maximum residue limits (MRLs) of diazinon (DZN), which are 0.04 µg/kg, 0.1 µg/L, and 0.04 µg/g, respectively, for soil (European Union), water (Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations/Food and Agriculture Organization) and vegetables (European Union). Hence, it is desirable to devise a reliable, low cost way of detecting DZN [90].

In a study, DZN was detected using CNTs modified carbon paste electrodes (CPE) in food samples. CPE is the most commonly used substrate material in electrochemical sensors due to its properties like high reproducibility, cost-effectiveness and easy modification [91]. Here, the preparation of the unmodified electrode was done by mixing graphite powder and paraffin oil whereas the modified electrode i.e. CNT/ CPE electrode was prepared by adding the appropriate amount of CNT to the carbon pate. Modifying electrode layers with nanoparticles such as CNTs for use in sensors has been chosen because of their astonishing properties. Additionally, CNTs exhibit excellent electrocatalytic performance as electrode materials due to their accurate electronic behaviour. FE-SEM images demonstrated that once CNTs were added to carbon paste, they were tubular and uniformly distributed on electrode surfaces. In CV results, the peak current for modified electrodes increased relative to bare CPEs, indicating that the presence of CNT improves CPE sensitivity and conductivity. EIS studies show that using CNT the impedance of the electrode decreases and the rate of electron transfer increases by reducing the charge transfer resistances. Thus, it was concluded that the prepared sensor gave satisfactory results for the detection of DZN in real samples [92].

A novel electrochemical sensor was developed to detect pesticide fenitrothion by modifying GCE using silica-coated, multi-walled partially oriented CNTs on which ruthenium phthalocyanine (SiO2/MWCNTs/RuPc) was immobilized. RuPc has excellent redox properties due to which it is widely used in the fabrication of electrochemical sensors. In addition to UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and XPS, SEM, TEM and DPV were used to characterize the hybrid SiO2/MWCNTs/RuPc material. LOD value was found to be 0.45 ppm using DPV and the sensitivity obtained was 0.0822 µAµM-1 mm-2. The developed electrode was successfully able to detect the fenitrothion pesticide in orange juice [85].

A recent study reports the use of an electrode ink that could be directly used for on-site detection of pesticide parathion in various vegetable samples. The electrode was made from MWCNTs and silicon carbide that was further stabilized by a layer of chitosan that acted as an adhesive as well. It was made from MWCNTs and silicon carbide that was further stabilized by a layer of chitosan that acted as an adhesive as well. SEM images revealed that in the absence of a chitosan layer loose topography was observed for the electrode. The fabricated electrode was efficient in detecting the parathion pesticide in vegetables like cabbage, sweet potato leaf, and cucumber [93].

Pesticide methyl parathion (MP), is found to be highly toxic due to which it can kill various plant pests. It is found that even a low concentration of MP can be very harmful. Therefore, its determination becomes very important. Electrochemical sensors are emerging as a boon for determining MPs. MWCNT enhances the detection of MPs as they have a larger surface area, higher conductivity, and its one-dimensional tube-like microstructure also supports the same. In a recent study electrochemical sensor was developed for the detection of MPs by modifying GCE with halloysite (Hal) nanotubes /MWCNTs. The nanocomposite was prepared by mixing Hal and MWCNT in a specific amount to form the suspension. The suspension was then dispersed on GCE and was allowed to dry using infrared for 15 min duration. An electrochemical study reveals that the Hal-MWCNTs/GCE sensor showed enhanced electrochemical performance when compared to the bare GCE. The prepared sensor gave satisfactory results with recovery rates of 98.8 % and 101.8 % [94]. Fig. 5 demonstrates the electrochemical detection of pesticide methyl parathion using Hal nanotubes /MWCNTs.

The CNT web is a novel manner of producing CNTs by drawing CNTs from a forest of aligned CNTs grown especially in a furnace [95]. A novel method was developed by modifying GCE using CNT-Web for detecting pesticide MP. As compared to bare GCE, these CNT webs have high electrocatalytic activity for MP adsorption and electrochemical detection by CV and DPV [96].

Similarly, N-doped carbon nano pipe-arrayed mesoporous carbon (N-CMK-5) was prepared by a hard template approach. The prepared electrochemical sensor was used for detecting pesticide amitrole. The study also aimed for investigating the effect of carbon mesostructure and nitrogen doping on electrochemical behaviour. Four mesoporous carbon nanomaterials - CMK-3 carbon, N-doped CMK-3 carbon, pure CMK-5 carbon, and as prepared N-CMK-5 carbon were synthesized for the study. It was inferred from the electrochemical studies done using CV and DPV that the prepared N-CMK-5 showed higher electrochemical activity as compared to other mesoporous carbon. The prepared sensor was successfully able to detect the pesticide amitrole in a real water sample [97].

Therefore, it can be concluded that the presence of CNTs structures increases the electrochemical signals generated during sensing, since they are electrochemically active and act as efficient electron channels [98]. The use of MWCNT-based composites as electrode materials has been widely spread. In addition to improving electrical conductivity, MWCNT also improves electrochemical activity towards analytes as a substrate material [99]. Table 5 represents the application of CNT for detecting different classes of pesticides.

Likewise pesticides, graphene is also considered promising electrode material for electrochemical detection of pesticides due to its electrical conduction properties, good mechanical sturdiness, and high theoretical specific surface area [100–103]. A subsequent section discusses the electrochemical detection of various pesticides with graphene and its oxide.

The carbamate compound carbofuran (CBF) is widely used in agriculture as a pesticide. Furthermore, its use can reduce the growth period and increase the yields of feed [104]. But due to its harmful effect on the nervous system, the use of pesticides is prohibited around the globe. In the fabrication of electrochemical sensors, nanostructured materials are proving to be of great importance due to their good catalytic properties and faster electron transfer rate. In one such study, modification of ITO was done followed by layer by layer (LbL) modification in two steps: (i)using LbL film of poly(diallyldimethyl ammonium) (PDDA) and GO-forming PDDA/ERGO modified ITO electrode. (ii) LbL film of MNP and poly(styrene sulfonate) PSS forming electrode ITO/(PDDA/ERGO)5/(MNP/PSS)5, where 5 represents the number of bilayers. A morphological study of the prepared electrode was done by combining microscopy with optical spectroscopy. It was inferred from the micro Raman characterization that ERGO covered the entire surface and the presence of Van der Waals and hydrophobic interaction between the analyte and the ERGO film. The electrochemical study done using DPV gave the LOD value of 0.407 μmol/ L with a sensitivity of 0.2543 (μA /cm2, μmol/ L). The prepared sensor was successfully used for the detection of CBF in samples of tap water and soil [105].

Boron nitride nanosheets are also known as “white graphene” which are 2-dimensional nanomaterials which are considered useful for electrochemical sensors due to their surface area and conductivity. An electrochemical sensor was developed for detecting pesticides like methyl parathion, diazinon, and chlorpyrifos simultaneously, using boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) and graphene oxide. TEM images showed that the formed BNQDs had uniform lateral sizes whereas few planar layers were observed for formed GO. Results obtained by EIS and CV study were completely in harmony with each other. The developed sensor was highly selective and sensitive for organophosphate pesticide detection in water and apple juice samples [106].

A significant advantage of GO is its surface area and controllable pore structure [107,108]. Reduced graphene oxide (rGO) possesses intrinsic catalytic properties as well as chemical stability, making it a good candidate as a detection material [109]. In a study, a highly selective and sensitive electrochemical sensor was fabricated using GO/magnesium oxide nanocomposite (GO/MgO) for analyzing nitrobenzene in water samples. Fig. 6. represents the preparation of the GO/MgO electrochemical sensor and its application. GO/MgO nanocomposite was synthesized hydrothermally. XRD study shows that the prepared composite showed the cubic crystalline structure, it also confirms the purity of the nanocomposite as no additional diffraction peaks were observed. Synergistic effects between the GO and MgO enhanced the performance of the modified sensor. The fabricated sensor gave satisfactory results for pesticide analysis in water samples for environment monitoring [108].

Since transistors have their amplification characteristics and high sensitivity, a new electrochemical detection method using transistors as electrodes is widely regarded as being a promising way of detecting and analyzing electrical signals. In a study, for the first time, a novel graphene electrochemical transistor (GECT) was successfully fabricated for detecting MP. Apart from exhibiting high accuracy in detecting MP in Chinese cabbage samples, the device also showed good selectivity for typical interferences, which demonstrated its practicality [110].

An interesting study on the electrochemical sensor was done for determining MP. The sensor was fabricated using nitrogen-doped holey graphene (N-HG). Doping of nitrogen into graphene not only increases the electrical conductivity but also enhances the ion and electron transfer rate. Holey graphene used in the study is derivative of graphene having nanopores on a basal plane. The study also investigated the effect of N-configurations on the kinetics of electron transfer which included three N-bonding configurations which were pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N. It was concluded that N-HG modified using high pyrrolic-N showed faster electron transfer rate and hence enhanced electrochemical property. The sensor was successfully able to detect MP in river water, vegetable, and food samples with a recoveries rate of 99%− 102% [111]. Table 6 represents the detection of different classes of pesticides using graphene and its oxide.

As discussed previously, MIP technique has outstanding selectiveness, specificity, and affinity to its target molecules. They also have the ability to distinguish molecules from other molecules that are having similar structures [112]. Due to their adsorption and selectivity, MIPs are considered useful electrochemical modifiers. They provide a viable alternative to traditional separation methods to overcome their shortcomings [113].

MIP can be synthesized via a non-covalent technique, pre-organized covalent approach, or semi-covalent approach. The first approach uses non-covalent interactions such as hydrogen bonds, Van der Waals forces, electrostatic interactions and hydrophobic interactions to form self-assembled molecules. In the covalent pre-organized approach, the monomer and template are connected through a reversible covalent interaction prior to polymerization. In the semi-covalent approach binding of monomers follows the covalent approach whereas rebinding occurs through the non-covalent approach [65]. A recent study shows that combing the MIP method with electrochemical methods provides the desirable outputs for the electrochemical detection of pesticides and antibiotics.

The MIP film can cause poor adhesion as well as low electrochemical signal. These problems can be resolved by adding nanomaterials to the electrode surface because they have a high surface-to-volume ratio and good conductivity. In one of the study a molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor was developed based on a glassy carbon electrode decorated with reduced GO and gold nanoparticles (rGO@Au) for the detection of carbofuran (CBF). It was observed that the introduction of rGO@Au into the imprinted membrane not only improved the electrochemical signal but also enhanced the recognization capacity of the sensor to detect CBF. The novel sensor was found to be highly sensitive and selective and also showed a wide linear range and lower detection limit for CBF detection[104]. Following the trend of the surface imprinting phenomena, a novel electrochemical sensor was developed for detecting diuron pesticides in river water samples. In addition to harming the environment, diuron can also adversely affect humans. The sensor was fabricated with MIP and carboxyl functionalized MWCNT-COOH. BET measurements were used to evaluate the surface characteristics of MIP and NIP samples (control polymers). Diauron electrochemical studies were conducted with MIP and MWCNT-COOH immobilized on a carbon paste electrode using SWV. The use of MWCNT enhanced the electrocatalytic activity of the prepared sensor. The sensitivity of MWCNT-COOH-MIP/CPE was 7.9 times more than bare CPE. The prepared sensor shared several advantages over the HPLC method in terms of cost, analysis time, sensitivity, and selectivity [114].

Profenofos pesticide is widely used in various countries like Thailand, Vietnam, India, Australia. BUT, profenofos residues negatively impact aquatic vertebrates and humans by inhibiting acetylcholinesterase (AChE), which can cause serious neurological complications [115]. WHO has classified PFF as a moderately toxic pesticide hence its detection becomes important. In a study, the fabrication of electrochemical sensors was done for detecting profenofos using 3D-CNTs@MIP/GCE. In this method, encapsulation of CNTs was done on silica for improving the electrocatalytic properties and sensitivity of the fabricated sensor. MIP printing on CNT provided a large number of imprinted site which increased its surface area further enhancing the electron transfer rate. [116]. Table 7 illustrates the various application of MIP-based electrochemical sensors for the detection of different pesticides.

An organophosphate pesticide, DZN is commonly used in agriculture to control insects, as well as in commercial and residential buildings for the control of cockroaches, silverfish, and ants. However, DZN causes several toxic effects in humans and animals, including the liver, thymus, spleen, lymph nodes, and blood cells. Thus, highly selective and reliable methods need to be developed for its detection. MIP nanoparticles were used in one study to develop an electrochemical sensor that could detect DZN pesticides selectively and sensitively. A DZN imprinted polymer nanoparticle was generated by suspension polymerization and then used to modify carbon paste electrode composition for preparing the sensor. Electrochemical measurements were carried out using CV and SWV analysis [117].

Apart from the carbon materials that are discussed before like carbon nanomaterials, graphene, and its oxide, MIP-based method for its application in electrochemical sensor some more carbon materials are also available for the same application. These include CNFs, CDs, silica gel, etc.

Fenitrothion is used for destroying insects and pests in cotton, rice, cereals, orchard fruits, and vegetables. The pesticide is extensively used in the agricultural field thus its accumulation in the environment is unavoidable. In a study detection of pesticides, fenitrothion was done by fabricating an electrochemical sensor using a nanosilver/dodecane modified electrode. Surface characterization was carried out using SEM, XRD, TEM and AFM, and quantitative determination was done using differential pulse voltammetry. It could be inferred from the EIS study that the deposition of silver nanoparticles lead to a reduction in resistance thereby increasing its conductivity. The modified electrode showed enhanced catalytic properties and was found to be highly stable and sensitive for many runs of the analysis [119].

CD due to their excellent properties like good solubility, less toxic nature, easy modification, and enhanced electrochemical activity is gaining much attention for electrochemical detection of pesticides [120–122]. In a study modification of GCE was done using CDs/ ZrO2 nanocomposite for detecting nitroaromatic organophosphorus pesticide MP. Interaction of CDs, and ZrO2 provided more contact area and also resulted in a faster electron transfer rate. The pesticide was efficiently detected in rice samples using adsorptive stripping voltammetry. Thus, the fabricated sensor gave a satisfactory result for pesticide detection [123].

In modern agriculture, PT is one of the best insecticides for controlling pests/insects on vegetables and fruits. But due to its detrimental effect on health and the environment, its use has been banned in many countries. Hence, it is crucial to develop techniques for detecting PT from real-world samples to ensure food safety, human health, and environmental protection. [86].

One of the most well-known and newly emerged types of carbon nanomaterials is CNFs. They stand out with unique properties when it comes to carbon nanomaterials [124]. A method was developed for determining PT pesticides in water, human urine, and vegetable samples using the electrochemical method. Interestingly, the electrochemical processes at electrode surfaces are governed by the physical characteristics of nanomaterials such as their size, shape, composition, surface area, and porosity. SPEs were modified using nickel oxide as a catalyst and PT was detected at pH 6. The results indicated that parathion molecules were highly adsorbed by NiO-SPE compared to inorganic salts and competitive pesticides [125].

Following the same trend, a novel electrochemical sensor was prepared by modifying CPE with the silica gel (SG/CPE) for detecting herbicide linuron.CV and SWV results showed that the fabricated sensor for the pesticide detection in soil and water samples was found to be highly sensitive and selective as the modified electrode showed enhanced electrocatalytic characteristics. The LOD was 3.94 nM whereas the LOQ was 13.1 nM [126]. Table 8 describes various miscellaneous carbon materials that are used for the electrochemical detection of pesticides.

The unavoidable use of pesticides and antibiotics is an alarming concern for human beings and the environment. Numerous studies have been conducted in the past few years that aimed at improving conventional pesticide and antibiotic detection methods. Therefore, electrochemical sensing has replaced conventional methods that require lengthy sample preparation, complex strategies, and lengthy analysis. Nanomaterial-based electrochemical sensing methods have demonstrated superior performance with rapid, accurate, and sensitive antibiotic detection. CNMs remarkable electrical and physical properties have allowed their application to the detection of analytes with high sensitivity and selectivity. Due to their low manufacturing costs, compact size, robustness, and power efficiency, these carbon-based electrochemical sensors make excellent portable field environment monitoring devices.

During this comprehensive review, electrochemical sensing strategies based on CV, SWV, DPV, amperometry and EIS were evaluated in various fruits, water and vegetables samples for detecting different classes of pesticides and antibiotics. The electrode is a key component of the whole sensor, therefore this review described the entire development process of the carbon based electrode for antibiotic and pesticides detection, as well as the mode of modification and materials. The review shows that carbon nanomaterials including carbon black, fibres, nanotubes, nanoparticles, graphene and its oxide proves to be a good candidate for electrochemical detection of both antibiotic and pesticides.

Despite the success obtained by electrochemical sensing techniques in detecting various classes of pesticides and antibiotics, many technical and scientific challenges remain. Some of the major challenges for the fabrication of electrochemical sensors for detecting antibiotics and pesticides are discussed below:
1.
There is a limitation to the ability of electrodes to detect all antibiotics and pesticides at the same time, so they are restricted to detecting one or a few antibiotics and pesticides at a time.
2.
Other antibiotics or pesticides may interfere with target antibiotics or pesticides, which is one of the challenges during sensor fabrication.
3.
Electrodes sensitivity needs to be enhanced for the smaller concentration of antibiotics and pesticide residue.
4.
In environmental applications, it is rare to obtain contamination-free samples, and contamination can adversely affect sensor performance.
5.
While these devices can achieve excellent selectivity and sensitivity in the laboratory, their performance in the field is uncertain.

The advances in nanoscience, electrochemistry, material science, and nanotechnology are expected to lead to advanced portable electrochemical devices quickly solving these challenges. As sensor technology advances, pesticide and antibiotics analysis will be more rapid and simpler, as well as portable and miniature. Newly developed methods should make the control of food safety and environmental resources easier.

This review will inspire new ideas in the field of fabricating novel surface modified electrodes for more precise, sensitive, and selective detection of antibiotics and pesticides by electrochemical techniques, developing electrodes that can be used in situ, fabricating a portable device that is practical in real life, and fabricating an ideal sensor that can be used on an industrial scale for the detection.
Smriti Mishra prepared and wrote the original draft. Sheelendra Pratap Singh, Pradip Kumar, Mohd. Akram Khan and Shiv Singh: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.
